revision.text:
'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

revision.comment: 1 revision imported: attributing page creation to myself as per first edit summary
revision.text:
'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. == References == {{Reflist}}

'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

revision.text:
'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.

== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

revision.text:
'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

revision.text:
'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

revision.text:
'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

revision.text:
'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

revision.text:
'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

revision.text:
'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

revision.text:
'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Q--q]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

revision.text:
'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. revision.text:
'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

revision.text:
'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

revision.comment: adding defaultsort to all of them
revision.text:
'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]][[en:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Tonegawa Zushi''' (利根川図志) is a geographical and cultural record of the mid- and lower reaches of the [[Tonegawa]] River, authored by [[Akamatsu Sōtan]] during the late [[Edo period]].
== Overview == The text was written in [[Ansei]] 2 (1855), as stated in the preface<ref name="jo">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938, pp. 28–33 (Preface and Notes)</ref>. According to *Tonegawa Zushi Chōchō*, the book was completed in October of Ansei 4 (1857) and distributed to contributors who assisted with the research<ref name="kawana157">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 157–161 (Completion of *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>. The [[bakufu]] officially approved its publication in Ansei 5 (1858)<ref name="kawana184">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 184–197 (Distribution Authorization)</ref>.
Akamatsu Sōtan (also known as Akamatsu Yoshitomo), a physician from [[Fukawa Village]] in [[Shimōsa Province]] (present-day [[Tone, Ibaraki]])<ref name="kawana30">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 30–39 (Author Overview)</ref>, authored the work. Born in Bunka 3 (1806), Sōtan was the second to bear this name in his family, following his father.
The work focuses on the mid- and lower reaches of the Tonegawa River, from its confluence with the [[Watarase River]] to its Pacific Ocean estuary, covering modern-day [[Koga, Ibaraki|Koga]] to [[Chōshi, Chiba|Chōshi, Chiba]]. It documents regional landmarks, historical sites, local products, customs, and geography, supplemented by numerous illustrations<ref name="kaidai">Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), pp. 3–14 (Introduction)</ref>.
Contributing artists include notable figures such as [[Katsushika Hokusai]], [[Katsushika Isai]], [[Yamagata Sosin]], [[Kojo Kiichi]], [[Gyokuransei Sadahide]], and [[Utagawa Hiroshige]]<ref name="kawana207">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 207–223 (Artists in *Tonegawa Zushi*)</ref>.
Sōtan intended to write a sequel focusing on the upper reaches of the Tonegawa River. Drafts of *Tonegawa Zushi Kōhen* and related notes remain, but Sōtan’s death in [[Bunkyū]] 2 (1862) at the age of 57 left the sequel unfinished<ref name="kawana198">Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010, pp. 198–202 (Work on Sequel and Death)</ref>.
== Structure == The text is organized into six volumes, covering topics such as transportation, weather, regional products, and significant locations, accompanied by a range of illustrations, including maps, sketches, and bird’s-eye views.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == - Akamatsu Sōtan, *Tonegawa Zushi* (edited by Yanagita Kunio, Iwanami Bunko), 1938; reprint 1987. - Kawana Noboru, *Hiden: Akamatsu Sōtan – Tonegawa Zushi no Dekiru Made*, Sairyūsha, 2010. - [https://www.lib.pref.saitama.jp/stplib_doc/data/d_conts/kicho/syosai/032.html Saitama Prefectural Library Digital Archive – *Tonegawa Zushi*]
